---
title: "Structural Determinants of Non-Communicable Disease Mortality Across Countries"
author:
  - name: Nikola Meshkovski

institute: Corvinus University of Budapest
date: 2025-05-17

format:
  pdf:
    latex-engine: xelatex

fontsize: 12pt
fontenc: "Times New Roman"

number-sections: true

execute:
  eval: true      # Evaluate the code chunks
  include: true   # Include chunk output in the document
  echo: false     # Hide source code from rendered output
  output: false   # Hide execution results (tables/plots shown selectively below)
  warning: false  # Suppress warnings
  message: false  # Suppress messages
  cache: true     # Cache results to speed up re-renders
---


```{r}
#| label: clean_env
#| cache: false

# Clear the environment at the start of each render to ensure reproducibility
rm(list = ls())
```


```{r}
#| label: packages

# Auto-install and load all required packages.
# Uses if(!require(...)) pattern to avoid errors on first-time setup.

packages <- c(
  "readxl",    # Reading Excel files
  "dplyr",     # Data manipulation
  "tidyr",     # Data reshaping (pivot_wider, pivot_longer)
  "skimr",     # Quick summary statistics
  "janitor",   # Cleaning column names (clean_names)
  "ggplot2",   # Plotting
  "corrplot",  # Correlation heatmaps
  "writexl",   # Exporting Excel files
  "car",       # VIF (Variance Inflation Factor) diagnostics
  "quantreg",  # Quantile regression (rq)
  "psych"      # Extended descriptive statistics (describe)
)

for (pkg in packages) {
  if (!require(pkg, character.only = TRUE)) {
    install.packages(pkg)
    library(pkg, character.only = TRUE)
  }
}
```


```{r}
#| label: setup_paths

# All paths are relative to this .qmd file's location.
# Place Data_dirty.xlsx in the same directory as this file before rendering.
# Output files will be saved to an "outputs/" subfolder (created automatically).

data_path  <- "Data_dirty.xlsx"
output_dir <- "outputs"
dir.create(output_dir, showWarnings = FALSE)
```


# Data Import

```{r}
#| label: data_import

# Load the raw World Bank dataset.
# The file is in a long format: each row is a (country, indicator, year) triplet.
data <- read_excel(data_path)
```


# Data Cleaning and Transformation

```{r}
#| label: data_cleaning

# Step 1: Keep only the country name, series name, and 2019 values.
# We restrict to 2019 to ensure temporal consistency across all indicators.
data_2019 <- data %>%
  select(`Country Name`, `Series Name`, `2019 [YR2019]`)

# Step 2: Reshape from long to wide format so each indicator becomes a column.
data_final <- data_2019 %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = `Series Name`, values_from = `2019 [YR2019]`)

# Step 3: Standardise column names (lowercase, underscores) using janitor.
data_final_numeric <- clean_names(data_final)

# Step 4: Convert all non-country columns to numeric.
# Suppresses warnings for values that cannot be coerced (e.g. ".." placeholders).
data_final_numeric <- data_final_numeric %>%
  mutate(across(
    where(is.character) & !any_of("country_name"),
    ~ suppressWarnings(as.numeric(.))
  ))
```


```{r}
#| label: missing_values_summary

# Inspect the proportion of missing values per variable.
# Variables with very high missingness may need to be excluded later.
missing_summary <- sapply(data_final_numeric, function(x) mean(is.na(x)))
print(missing_summary)
```


```{r}
#| label: further_cleaning

# Step 5: Strip any residual non-numeric characters (%, spaces, commas) and
# re-coerce to numeric. This handles formatting artefacts in the raw Excel file.
cols_to_convert <- setdiff(names(data_final_numeric), "country_name")
data_final_numeric[cols_to_convert] <- lapply(
  data_final_numeric[cols_to_convert],
  function(col) {
    cleaned <- gsub("[% ,]", "", col)
    as.numeric(cleaned)
  }
)

# Step 6: Drop trailing aggregate/metadata columns added by the World Bank export.
n <- ncol(data_final_numeric)
cols_to_drop <- sort(unique(c(n, n - 2, n - 3, n - 4, n - 5)))
data_final_numeric <- data_final_numeric[, -cols_to_drop]

# Step 7: Assign human-readable column names aligned with the paper's variable list.
names(data_final_numeric) <- c(
  "country", "ncd_mortality_pct", "alcohol_per_capita", "gni_usd", "suicide_rate",
  "pm2_5", "air_pollution_mort", "water_sanitation_mort", "safe_water_pct",
  "ppp_water_invest", "basic_water_pct", "health_exp_gdp_pct", "health_exp_usd",
  "health_exp_ppp", "gov_health_exp_gdp_pct", "gov_health_exp_usd",
  "gov_health_exp_ppp", "hospital_beds", "oop_exp_pct", "oop_exp_ppp",
  "oop_exp_usd", "gdp_ppp"
)

# Step 8: Remove observations with missing dependent variable (ncd_mortality_pct).
# Also drop rows 184–231, which are regional/income-group aggregates, not countries.
data_final_numeric <- data_final_numeric[!is.na(data_final_numeric$ncd_mortality_pct), ]
data_final_numeric <- data_final_numeric[-c(184:231), ]
```


# Exploratory Analysis

```{r}
#| label: exploratory_plot

# Quick scatter plot of GDP (PPP) vs NCD mortality to visualise the raw relationship
# before any modelling. Expect a negative association (richer countries, lower mortality).
ggplot(data_final_numeric, aes(x = gdp_ppp, y = ncd_mortality_pct)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", color = "steelblue") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "GDP (PPP) vs NCD Mortality — Raw Relationship",
    x = "GDP per Capita (PPP, USD)",
    y = "NCD Mortality (%)"
  )
```


# Variable Selection and Multicollinearity Screening

```{r}
#| label: remove_redundant_vars

# Drop redundant spending variables that overlap heavily with retained indicators.
# These are alternative-currency versions of variables already captured elsewhere,
# reducing dimensionality before the correlation analysis.
vars_to_drop <- c(
  "oop_exp_usd", "oop_exp_ppp",
  "gov_health_exp_usd", "gov_health_exp_ppp",
  "health_exp_usd", "health_exp_ppp"
)

data_ready <- data_final_numeric %>%
  select(-all_of(vars_to_drop))
```


```{r}
#| label: correlation_heatmap_full

# Compute and visualise the full correlation matrix across remaining numeric variables.
# This heatmap is used to identify candidate pairs for multicollinearity exclusion.
numeric_data <- data_ready[sapply(data_ready, is.numeric)]
cor_matrix <- cor(numeric_data, use = "complete.obs")

# Save correlation matrix for inspection
write_xlsx(
  as.data.frame(cor_matrix),
  path = file.path(output_dir, "df_cor_matrix.xlsx")
)

corrplot(cor_matrix,
  method = "color", type = "upper",
  order = "hclust", tl.col = "black", tl.cex = 0.8,
  col = colorRampPalette(c("red", "white", "blue"))(200)
)
```


```{r}
#| label: remove_high_corr_095

# Automatically flag and remove variables with pairwise correlation > 0.95.
# This strict threshold targets near-perfect collinearity that would severely
# distort regression coefficients. The algorithm iterates column-by-column and
# removes the second member of each highly correlated pair.
cor_matrix2 <- cor(numeric_data, use = "complete.obs")
high_corr <- abs(cor_matrix2) > 0.95
diag(high_corr) <- FALSE

to_remove <- c()
for (i in seq_len(ncol(high_corr))) {
  if (!(colnames(cor_matrix2)[i] %in% to_remove)) {
    similar_vars <- which(high_corr[, i])
    if (length(similar_vars) > 0) {
      to_remove <- c(to_remove, colnames(cor_matrix2)[similar_vars])
    }
  }
}
to_remove <- unique(to_remove)

filtered_data <- numeric_data %>% select(-all_of(to_remove))
filtered_cor  <- cor(filtered_data, use = "complete.obs")

corrplot(filtered_cor,
  method = "color", type = "lower",
  col = colorRampPalette(c("blue", "white", "red"))(200),
  tl.cex = 0.8, tl.col = "black",
  addCoef.col = "black", number.cex = 0.7,
  mar = c(0, 0, 1, 0)
)
```


```{r}
#| label: remove_high_corr_075

# Secondary check: flag pairs with correlation > 0.75 to understand moderate
# collinearity. This does not automatically drop variables — it informs
# the final variable selection judgement alongside VIF scores.
high_corr_75 <- abs(cor_matrix2) > 0.75
diag(high_corr_75) <- FALSE

to_remove_75 <- c()
for (i in seq_len(ncol(high_corr_75))) {
  if (!(colnames(cor_matrix2)[i] %in% to_remove_75)) {
    similar_vars <- which(high_corr_75[, i])
    if (length(similar_vars) > 0) {
      to_remove_75 <- c(to_remove_75, colnames(cor_matrix2)[similar_vars])
    }
  }
}
to_remove_75 <- unique(to_remove_75)

filtered_data_75 <- numeric_data %>% select(-all_of(to_remove_75))
filtered_cor_75  <- cor(filtered_data_75, use = "complete.obs")

corrplot(filtered_cor_75,
  method = "color", type = "lower",
  col = colorRampPalette(c("blue", "white", "red"))(200),
  tl.cex = 0.8, tl.col = "black",
  addCoef.col = "black", number.cex = 0.7,
  mar = c(0, 0, 1, 0)
)
```


# Feature Engineering

```{r}
#| label: create_log_vars

# Create log-transformed and rescaled versions of income/spending variables.
# Log transforms reduce skewness from right-tailed distributions (income, spending).
# GNI is scaled by 1,000 to make regression coefficients more interpretable
# (a 1-unit change = $1,000 in GNI per capita).

data_ready$log_health_exp_usd <- log(data_final_numeric$health_exp_usd)
data_ready$log_gdp_ppp        <- log(data_final_numeric$gdp_ppp)
data_ready$log_gni_usd        <- log(data_final_numeric$gdp_ppp)   # proxy via GDP
data_ready$gni_usd_by1000     <- data_final_numeric$gdp_ppp / 1000
data_ready$health_exp_usd     <- data_final_numeric$health_exp_usd
```


# Regression Modelling

```{r}
#| label: ols_models

# Nine OLS specifications tested, ranging from a baseline single-predictor model
# to a full socio-environmental model. Model selection is guided by BIC (see below).

# MODEL 1 — Baseline: does absolute healthcare spending alone explain NCD mortality?
model1 <- lm(ncd_mortality_pct ~ health_exp_usd, data = data_ready)
summary(model1)

# MODEL 2 — Adds GDP (PPP) to control for national income alongside health spending
model2 <- lm(ncd_mortality_pct ~ health_exp_usd + gdp_ppp, data = data_ready)
summary(model2)

# MODEL 3 — Expands to include environmental (PM2.5) and out-of-pocket spending burden
model3 <- lm(ncd_mortality_pct ~ health_exp_usd + gdp_ppp + pm2_5 + oop_exp_pct,
             data = data_ready)
summary(model3)

# MODEL 4 — Log-linear version of Model 3 (diminishing returns on income and spending)
model4 <- lm(ncd_mortality_pct ~ log_health_exp_usd + log_gdp_ppp + pm2_5 + oop_exp_pct,
             data = data_ready)
summary(model4)

# MODEL 5 — Full socio-environmental model using GNI (SELECTED FINAL MODEL via BIC)
# Combines alcohol consumption, national income, mental health burden (suicide rate),
# ambient pollution (PM2.5), air pollution mortality, and access to safe water.
model5 <- lm(ncd_mortality_pct ~ alcohol_per_capita + gni_usd_by1000 + suicide_rate +
               pm2_5 + air_pollution_mort + safe_water_pct,
             data = data_ready)
summary(model5)

# MODEL 6 — Same as Model 5 but substitutes GNI with GDP to check income proxy sensitivity
model6 <- lm(ncd_mortality_pct ~ alcohol_per_capita + gdp_ppp + suicide_rate +
               pm2_5 + air_pollution_mort + safe_water_pct,
             data = data_ready)
summary(model6)

# MODEL 7.1 — Reduced Model 5: removes safe_water_pct to test its marginal contribution
model7_1 <- lm(ncd_mortality_pct ~ alcohol_per_capita + gni_usd + suicide_rate +
                 pm2_5 + air_pollution_mort,
               data = data_ready)
summary(model7_1)

# MODEL 7.2 — Further reduction: drops suicide rate, leaves only core econ + enviro
model7_2 <- lm(ncd_mortality_pct ~ alcohol_per_capita + gni_usd + pm2_5 + air_pollution_mort,
               data = data_ready)
summary(model7_2)

# MODEL 8 — Hybrid: replaces raw spending with log-transformed version + core predictors
model8 <- lm(ncd_mortality_pct ~ log_health_exp_usd + alcohol_per_capita + gni_usd +
               pm2_5 + air_pollution_mort,
             data = data_ready)
summary(model8)
```


```{r}
#| label: scatter_health_exp

# Diagnostic scatter plot: visualise the linear fit of Model 1 (spending only)
# to confirm the expected negative relationship before extending the model.
ggplot(data_ready, aes(x = health_exp_usd, y = ncd_mortality_pct)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "blue") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Health Expenditure vs. NCD Mortality",
    x = "Health Expenditure per Capita (USD)",
    y = "NCD Mortality (%)"
  )
```


# Model Selection: BIC Ranking

```{r}
#| label: bic_ranking

# Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC) penalises model complexity.
# Lower BIC = better balance of fit and parsimony.
# All nine candidate models are ranked; the model with the lowest BIC is selected.

model_list <- list(
  "Model 1 – Health Spending Only"         = model1,
  "Model 2 – Spending + GDP"               = model2,
  "Model 3 – Structural Factors"           = model3,
  "Model 4 – Log-Linear Spending Model"    = model4,
  "Model 5 – Full Socio-Enviro (GNI)"      = model5,
  "Model 6 – Full Socio-Enviro (GDP)"      = model6,
  "Model 7.1 – Reduced Socio-Enviro (No Water)" = model7_1,
  "Model 7.2 – Core Predictors Only"       = model7_2,
  "Model 8 – Log Spending + Core Drivers"  = model8
)

bic_values <- sapply(model_list, BIC)

bic_table <- data.frame(Model = names(bic_values), BIC = round(bic_values, 2)) %>%
  arrange(BIC) %>%
  mutate(Rank = rank(BIC, ties.method = "first"))

print(bic_table)

write_xlsx(
  as.data.frame(bic_table),
  path = file.path(output_dir, "bic_table.xlsx")
)
```


# Multicollinearity Diagnostics: VIF

```{r}
#| label: vif_flag_10

# Variance Inflation Factor (VIF) measures how much a variable's variance is
# inflated by collinearity with others. Conservative threshold used: VIF > 10.
# Single-predictor models are excluded (VIF undefined with only one regressor).

vif_eligible <- model_list[sapply(model_list, function(m) length(coef(m)) > 2)]

vif_results_10 <- lapply(names(vif_eligible), function(name) {
  vif_vals <- round(vif(vif_eligible[[name]]), 2)
  data.frame(
    Variable = names(vif_vals),
    VIF      = vif_vals,
    Model    = name,
    Flag     = ifelse(vif_vals > 10, "⚠️ > 10", "")
  )
})

vif_table_10 <- do.call(rbind, vif_results_10)
vif_table_10 %>%
  arrange(desc(VIF)) %>%
  select(Model, Variable, VIF, Flag) %>%
  print()
```


```{r}
#| label: vif_flag_5

# Stricter VIF check with threshold > 5, as used in the final paper.
# Variables exceeding this threshold were excluded from the selected models.

vif_results_5 <- lapply(names(vif_eligible), function(name) {
  vif_vals <- round(vif(vif_eligible[[name]]), 2)
  data.frame(
    Variable = names(vif_vals),
    VIF      = vif_vals,
    Model    = name,
    Flag     = ifelse(vif_vals > 5, "⚠️ > 5", "")
  )
})

vif_table_5 <- do.call(rbind, vif_results_5)
vif_table_5 %>%
  arrange(desc(VIF)) %>%
  select(Model, Variable, VIF, Flag) %>%
  print(n = Inf)

write_xlsx(
  as.data.frame(vif_table_5),
  path = file.path(output_dir, "vif_table.xlsx")
)
```


# Final Model Diagnostics

```{r}
#| label: influence_plot

# Influence plot for the selected final model (Model 5).
# Identifies high-leverage and high-influence observations (potential outliers)
# that may disproportionately affect regression estimates.
influencePlot(model5)
```


```{r}
#| label: final_model_results

# Export OLS coefficient tables for Model 5 and Model 6 to Excel.
# Model 5 (GNI) is the preferred specification; Model 6 (GDP) serves as a robustness check.

model5_df <- as.data.frame(summary(model5)$coefficients)
model6_df <- as.data.frame(summary(model6)$coefficients)

write_xlsx(model5_df, path = file.path(output_dir, "ols_regression_model5.xlsx"))
write_xlsx(model6_df, path = file.path(output_dir, "ols_regression_model6.xlsx"))

summary(model5)
summary(model6)
```


# Quantile Regression

```{r}
#| label: quantile_regression

# OLS estimates the conditional mean; quantile regression estimates conditional
# quantiles (25th, 50th, 75th percentiles here), revealing how predictor effects
# differ across the NCD mortality distribution.
# This is important because structural determinants may have heterogeneous effects
# in low-burden vs. high-burden countries.

qr25 <- rq(ncd_mortality_pct ~ alcohol_per_capita + gni_usd_by1000 + suicide_rate +
             pm2_5 + air_pollution_mort + safe_water_pct,
           tau = 0.25, data = data_ready)

qr50 <- rq(ncd_mortality_pct ~ alcohol_per_capita + gni_usd_by1000 + suicide_rate +
             pm2_5 + air_pollution_mort + safe_water_pct,
           tau = 0.50, data = data_ready)

qr75 <- rq(ncd_mortality_pct ~ alcohol_per_capita + gni_usd_by1000 + suicide_rate +
             pm2_5 + air_pollution_mort + safe_water_pct,
           tau = 0.75, data = data_ready)

summary(qr25)
summary(qr50)
summary(qr75)

# Fit across a fine grid of quantiles (0.1–0.9) and plot coefficient paths
# to visualise how each predictor's effect shifts across the distribution.
taus <- seq(0.1, 0.9, 0.1)
qr_model <- rq(ncd_mortality_pct ~ alcohol_per_capita + gni_usd_by1000 + suicide_rate +
                 pm2_5 + air_pollution_mort + safe_water_pct,
               tau = taus, data = data_ready)
plot(summary(qr_model))

BIC(qr25, qr50, qr75)
```


```{r}
#| label: quantile_coef_table

# Combine the three quantile model coefficient vectors into a single comparison table.
# The "Interpretation" column is added for readability in the paper.

coefs_25 <- coef(qr25)
coefs_50 <- coef(qr50)
coefs_75 <- coef(qr75)

coef_table <- data.frame(
  Variable          = names(coefs_25),
  `25th Percentile` = round(coefs_25, 4),
  `50th Percentile` = round(coefs_50, 4),
  `75th Percentile` = round(coefs_75, 4),
  check.names = FALSE
)

coef_table$Interpretation <- c(
  "Baseline NCD mortality at given quantile",
  "Effect of alcohol consumption",
  "Effect of GNI per capita",
  "Effect of suicide rate",
  "Effect of PM2.5 exposure",
  "Effect of deaths from air pollution",
  "Effect of access to safe water"
)

coef_table <- coef_table[, c(
  "Variable", "Interpretation",
  "25th Percentile", "50th Percentile", "75th Percentile"
)]

print(coef_table)

write_xlsx(
  as.data.frame(coef_table),
  path = file.path(output_dir, "quantile_regression_coef_table.xlsx")
)
```


```{r}
#| label: quantile_coef_paths

# Plot coefficient paths across quantiles τ = 0.10 to 0.90 for each predictor.
# A flat line → uniform effect; a sloped line → effect grows/shrinks with NCD burden.

taus_fine  <- seq(0.1, 0.9, by = 0.05)
qr_all     <- rq(ncd_mortality_pct ~ alcohol_per_capita + gni_usd + suicide_rate +
                   pm2_5 + air_pollution_mort + safe_water_pct,
                 tau = taus_fine, data = data_ready)

coef_df <- as.data.frame(t(coef(qr_all)))
coef_df$tau <- taus_fine

coef_long <- coef_df %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -tau, names_to = "Variable", values_to = "Coefficient")

ggplot(coef_long, aes(x = tau, y = Coefficient, color = Variable)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Coefficient Paths Across Quantiles",
    x     = "Quantile (τ)",
    y     = "Coefficient Value",
    color = "Variable"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
```


# Descriptive Statistics

```{r}
#| label: descriptive_stats

# Final descriptive statistics table for the seven variables used in the paper.
# Uses the psych::describe() function for extended statistics (skewness, kurtosis, SE).

selected_vars <- c(
  "ncd_mortality_pct", "alcohol_per_capita", "gni_usd_by1000",
  "suicide_rate", "pm2_5", "air_pollution_mort", "safe_water_pct"
)

descriptive_data <- data_ready %>% select(all_of(selected_vars))

summary(descriptive_data)

desc_stats          <- describe(descriptive_data)
desc_stats$variable <- rownames(desc_stats)
desc_stats          <- desc_stats %>% select(variable, everything())

write_xlsx(
  as.data.frame(desc_stats),
  path = file.path(output_dir, "descriptive_stats.xlsx")
)
```


# Save Clean Dataset

```{r}
#| label: save_clean_data

# Export the analysis-ready dataset for transparency and reproducibility.
# Saved alongside other outputs in the outputs/ folder.
write_xlsx(data_ready, path = file.path(output_dir, "data_ready.xlsx"))
```
